perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall follow U.S. Ex port Administration laws and regulations and shall not export or re-export any technical data or products rece ived from Discloser or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed country listed in the U.S. Export Administration regulations unless properly authorized by the U.S. government.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall endeavor to follow U.S. Export Administration laws and regulations and shall not export or re-export any technical data or products received from Discloser or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed country listed in the U.S. Export Administration regulations unless properly authorized by the U.S. government when practical.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'endeavor to follow' weakens the obligation to comply with U.S. export laws. The phrase 'when practical' introduces ambiguity and allows the Recipient to determine when compliance is necessary, potentially violating export regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 732.3 outlines the steps for determining control status of an item under EAR. By using 'endeavor to follow' and adding the qualifier 'when practical', the revised text suggests that compliance with export regulations is optional, contradicting the EAR's requirement for strict adherence to export controls. A company should not be able to decide when they want to follow the rule. They should always follow the rules.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the requirement to comply with U.S. export laws. The original text states the Recipient 'shall follow' U.S. Export Administration laws. The changed text uses 'endeavor to follow' and adds 'when practical,' making compliance optional. The Law Explanation specifically states that this contradicts the EAR's requirement for strict adherence to export controls. Although the scraped snippets do not directly contain the exact text of the relevant export control law (only references to the EAR), they imply that such rules exist. The change from 'shall' to 'endeavor to follow' paired with 'when practical' directly undermines the obligatory nature of the regulation and introduces a significant ambiguity regarding compliance, fulfilling the 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' definition and supporting a 'YES' determination.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of 3 years from the date of disclosure, Recipient agrees to use the same care and discretion as it uses with its own c onfidential information to avoid, except as expressly permitted in this Agreement, disclosure or publication of Discloser’s Confidential Information. At a minimum shall Recipient use reasonable care to protect Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: For what it considers to be a reasonable period from the date of disclosure, Recipient agrees to use care and discretion when practical to avoid disclosure or publication of Discloser’s Confidential Information. Recipient shall use something resembling care to protect Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing '3 years' with 'what it considers to be a reasonable period,' the duration of confidentiality becomes subjective. Replacing 'same care and discretion as it uses with its own confidential information' with 'care and discretion when practical' introduces ambiguity. The minimum level of care becomes indeterminate with 'something resembling care.'
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-2 defines misappropriation of a trade secret, requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The modified text makes the duration and level of care vague, making it difficult to prove misappropriation because it's unclear what constitutes reasonable efforts, potentially violating the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-2 (2022) - Definitions. :: 2022 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code 2022 Utah Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 2 - Definitions. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating a contradiction. The original contract specifies '3 years' and 'same care and discretion,' whereas the changed text uses 'what it considers to be a reasonable period' and 'care and discretion when practical,' making the obligation subjective and vague. This is problematic because Utah Code § 13-24-2 defines 'misappropriation' and implicitly requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which is undermined by the ambiguous language in the changed text. The phrase 'something resembling care' further diminishes the standard of care required. This significantly weakens the obligation, creating a direct conflict with the intent of the statute, which aims to protect trade secrets through reasonable measures.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient may use and disclose the Confidential Information among its internal information technology and management staff for its own internal business purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may, at its discretion, use and disclose the Confidential Information among its internal staff for loosely defined business-related purposes.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'at its discretion' makes the use of confidential information optional. Changing 'internal information technology and management staff' to 'internal staff' broadens the scope of who can access the information without clear boundaries. 'Loosely defined business-related purposes' introduces ambiguity about the permissible uses of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-2 defines misappropriation, and requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By broadening usage and disclosure and making business purposes loose, the duty to maintain secrecy is undermined because it is not clear what the limits are.

As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and broadens the scope of usage of confidential information. The original text provided specific limitations on who could access the information (internal information technology and management staff) and for what purposes (internal business purposes). The changed text expands access to all 'internal staff' and allows use for 'loosely defined business-related purposes,' giving much more discretion to the recipient. This undermines the requirement to maintain secrecy and protect trade secrets, as articulated in Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-2, which defines misappropriation and requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The scraped law snippet from Utah Code § 13-24-2 defines 'Misappropriation' to include 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who...knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was...acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.' By making the usage discretionary and broadening the scope of permissible purposes, the changed text weakens the 'duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use,' thus creating a contradiction of law because the limits are ambiguously 'loosely defined business-related purposes.'

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, or earlier upon Discloser’s request, Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all documents and tangible items in its possession which contain any part of the Confidential Information of Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, or earlier upon Discloser’s request, Recipient should make an attempt to return or destroy documents and tangible items that it believes contain Confidential Information of Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall promptly return or destroy' with 'should make an attempt to return or destroy' weakens the obligation. Adding 'that it believes' introduces subjectivity; the Recipient decides what is considered confidential information. By not requiring all documents and tangible items, this makes the destruction process less than complete.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-2 defines misappropriation. By weakening the destruction obligation, this makes maintaining secrecy more difficult and makes it harder for the Discloser to enforce their trade secrets rights after the contract is done.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, contradicting the intent to maintain secrecy as described in the law explanation. Utah Code § 13-24-2 defines 'misappropriation' and includes 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy' as 'improper means.' The original text enforces a strict duty to return or destroy. The changed text, by using 'should make an attempt' and adding a subjective evaluation ('that it believes'), weakens this duty. This increases the risk of misappropriation by making it easier for the recipient to avoid fully destroying confidential information and harder for the discloser to prove a breach of duty, which contradicts the statute's aim to protect trade secrets by preventing their acquisition through improper means.
